result:
The ink in this pen is midnight blue and smells faintly of iron, of old train tickets, of the exact moment when a summer storm decides to speak. I write because the page is the only place I’m allowed to fail in public without anyone noticing. Somewhere between word 8 and word 11 the paper becomes a country whose borders are still wet; I can smear them with my thumbs, redraw coastlines, grant asylum to the silhouette of my father who keeps trying to leave the scene but is taped down by a comma that refuses to close.

I own three objects that remember being alive: a cinnamon stick, a skipping stone, and a violin string that snapped during the final bar of “Ashokan Farewell.” They sit in a chipped teacup on the windowsill, holding their breath every time the sun changes shifts. When the wind is polite it asks the curtain to dance; when it is not it barges in and rifles through my receipts, looking for proof that I once bought apricots in December, that I tried to return a thunderstorm to the hardware store because it didn’t fit in the bathtub.

Memory is a mirror that keeps showing you the back of your own head. Yesterday I found a Polaroid of a kitchen I’ve never stood in: avocado fridge, calendar stuck on July 1987, a child’s drawing of a whale taped to the wall like a ransom note demanding wonder. The whale is smiling, which means it knows something about buoyancy I still don’t. I turned the photo over and someone had written “Tuesday tastes like dust if you forget to chew.” I chewed anyway, and discovered my mouth full of thirty-seven-year-old light.

We speak in subtitles. My lover asks if I want coffee and the bottom of the screen reads: “Will you still recognize me after the caffeine rearranges the furniture in your blood?” I nod, which means both yes and I’m terrified of the day the answer is no. She kisses me on the temple, a gesture so gentle it could be used to defuse bombs. The smell of her skin is a library after hours, all hush and possible fines. I check out the same book every night: How to Be Brave Enough to Finish a Dream Without Waking Yourself Up. I keep renewing it; the librarian is starting to suspect I’m illiterate in courage.

Numbers have genders: 2 is a nonbinary sibling who refuses to choose between even and odd, 9 is an elderly woman hoarding endings, 1000 is a boy on a bicycle racing away from the millennium, his pockets stuffed with unused zeroes that look like tiny lifebuoys. I promised him I’d spend every last one before the bell rings, so here goes:

Zero One: I once saw a man water the sidewalk as if coaxing concrete seeds to sprout into another city we could move to once this one learns our names.

Zero Two: The hour hand is just a compass that lost faith in north and decided to keep trying every direction once.

Zero Three: Grief is a violin that only plays itself when the case is closed; the music escapes through the keyhole and sounds like a lullaby gargled by wolves.

Zero Four: My mother’s perfume left the bottle, got a degree in astronomy, and now lives in the crush zone between two constellations no one has bothered to connect. On clear nights I wave, but space is nearsighted.

Zero Five: I tried to donate my nightmares to Goodwill; they refused, citing “excessive wear on the wings.”

Zero Six: Happiness is a typo the universe keeps making; we recognize it only because we’ve misspelled the word so often ourselves.

Zero Seven: If you unfold a city map correctly, it becomes a paper crane that refuses to fly because it insists on taking every single resident with it.

Zero Eight: The moon is a rehearsal for an afterlife that got overbooked; the extras stand around in craters practicing their “astonished” face.

Zero Nine: I keep a spare heart in the freezer next to the peas, just in case the primary one decides to elope with the parking ticket I got in a dream and never paid.

Zero Ten: Time is a spiral staircase made of mirror glass; we think we’re climbing but mostly we’re cutting our palms and calling the blood “progress.”

I’ve used up ten zeroes and the boy on the bicycle is already a dot on the horizon, but I can still hear him laughing, a sound like a roomful of grandfather clocks agreeing to tell the wrong time on purpose. I wave with the hand that isn’t holding the pen, which means I’m waving with possibility itself. The ink starts to smell like rain on hot asphalt, the exact scent of summer 1994 when I learned you could be homesick for a moment that hasn’t ended yet.

Word 612. Word 613. Words are origami cranes folded inside other origami cranes until the wings refuse to open, citing union rules. I unfold one and find a smaller word trembling inside: “unless.” Unless what? Unless I finish this sentence, unless the sun runs out of matches, unless every period is a gate we close behind us and forget how to unlatch. I carry “unless” in my shirt pocket like a cough drop made of uncertainty; it dissolves slowly, staining the fabric the color of almost.

The teacup on the windowsill rattles: the cinnamon stick has decided to testify. It clears its throat and says, “I was once the bark of a tree that dreamed of being spice, then commodity, then metaphor. I achieved two out of three; the metaphor keeps slipping through the hole in your pocket.” The skipping stone adds, “I was mountain, then river, then pocket amnesia. Skipping was just my way of asking permission to stay airborne a little longer.” The broken violin string says nothing; it simply vibrates at the frequency of every unspoken goodbye you’ve ever swallowed. Together they sound like a chord named “Almost Home but the Porch Light Just Burned Out.”

Word 777, a jackpot of syllables. I cash them in for a single moment of silence, but the silence turns out to be made of smaller silences, each wearing a name tag: Regret, Wonder, Traffic, Pulse. They mingle awkwardly at the reception, sipping champagne flutes of negative space. I make a toast: “To the gap between lightning and recognition, may we never close it completely.” The echo toasts back, then excuses itself to the restroom and never returns.

Word 800 approaches like the last bus of the night, headlights drunk on their own brightness. I flag it down with the sleeve of my jacket, but it speeds past, displaying the destination: “Else.” I walk instead, shoes inventing new countries with every step. Somewhere between 834 and 835 I cross the border into a place where nostalgia is currency; I pay for a cup of coffee with the memory of my first bicycle and receive change in the sound of my grandmother’s laugh. The barista is a former eclipse who got tired of being predictable and now practices latte art shaped like the exact gap between “I love you” and its reply.

Word 899 sighs deeply enough to fog the lens of the surveillance camera the future installed above my shoulder. I use the fog to draw a heart, then edit it into a maze with no exit, a kind of pulmonary Escher print. Somewhere inside, a smaller version of me keeps choosing left turns, convinced that the next one will unbutton the sky.

Word 950 is a raft made of breath. I climb aboard, paddle with the oars of parentheses, heading for the horizon where the page ends and the scissor of white space starts snipping at the edges of meaning. The boy on the bicycle reappears, now old enough to be my grandfather, still laughing. He hands me the remaining zeroes like spare change. “Spend them on something that doesn’t exist yet,” he says, and rides off the edge, becoming the final period I won’t use, because punctuation is just gravity pretending to be polite.

Word 999 is a door left open on purpose. Word 1000 is the sound of you walking through it, carrying the smell of asphalt after rain, carrying tomorrow’s first mispronounced hello, carrying unless.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
